name: Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo Loop
name_original: Cueva Huerta, Puerto de Ventana, Puerto de San Lorenzo — circular desde Sanmartín
description: This difficult 37.8-mile touring cycling loop tackles the challenging Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo passes in Asturias.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Teverga, nestled in the Principality of Asturias, offers routes through rugged mountain terrain and protected natural parks. The region is characterized by immense limestone masses, deep valleys, and extensive deciduous forests of beech, oak, and chestnut. Numerous mountain passes, such as Ventana Pass and San Lorenzo Pass, provide challenging ascents and connections to other areas. Teverga forms part of the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park, a Biosphere Reserve known for its rich ecosystems. Best touring cycling routes around Teverga The most popular touring cycling route is Samartín, Puerto San Lorenzo, Alto de La Farrapona, Puerto de Ventana, Cueva Huerta — circular through the Parque Natural de Somiedo, a 48.3 miles (77.7 km) trail that takes 6 hours 31 minutes to complete. This difficult route navigates through the Parque Natural de Somiedo, incorporating several significant mountain passes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Senda del Oso and Cruz de Linares Loop, a difficult 34.6 miles (55.7 km) path. This route combines the well-known Senda del Oso, a former railway line, with additional climbs and varied landscapes. Local touring cyclists also love the Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo Loop, a 37.8 miles (60.8 km) trail leading through challenging mountain passes, often completed in about 5 hours 33 minutes. Touring cycling around Teverga is defined by rugged mountain passes, dense beech forests, and the dramatic limestone formations of the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park. The network offers options for various ability levels, from accessible paths like the Senda del Oso to demanding mountain ascents. The routes in Teverga are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from more than 60 reviews. More than 1000 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Teverga's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Teverga?
A: Teverga offers a diverse network of over 35 touring cycling routes. While many are challenging, navigating rugged mountain passes and significant elevation changes, there are also more accessible options like the Senda del Oso.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Teverga?
A: Teverga's touring cycling routes are characterized by rugged mountain terrain, immense limestone masses, and deep valleys. You'll encounter extensive deciduous forests of beech, oak, and chestnut, especially within the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park. Many routes feature challenging ascents over mountain passes like Ventana Pass and San Lorenzo Pass, but also include flatter sections, such as the former railway line of the Senda del Oso.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Teverga?
A: Yes, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1041800006/senda-del-oso-and-cruz-de-linares-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Senda del Oso&lt;/a&gt; is particularly well-suited for families. This route follows a disused mining railway line, offering a relatively flat and accessible path through picturesque landscapes, small villages, and well-lit tunnels. Bicycle and Segway hire is available locally, making it convenient for all ages.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the touring cycling routes in Teverga?
A: The Senda del Oso is generally considered dog-friendly, allowing you to enjoy the ride with your canine companion. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash and ensure they are well-behaved, especially in areas where you might encounter other cyclists or wildlife. For other routes, check local regulations within the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see along the cycling routes?
A: Teverga's routes offer access to several natural and cultural highlights. You can cycle past the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2949141" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Desfiladero de Peñas Juntas on the Senda del Oso&lt;/a&gt;, or explore the extensive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3592942" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Huerta Cave&lt;/a&gt;, a Natural Monument. Mountain passes like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/176266" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ventana Pass&lt;/a&gt; offer breathtaking views. The region is also home to the Xiblu waterfall, accessible via a didactic itinerary through the Montegrande Beech Forest.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Teverga?
A: Yes, Teverga offers several excellent circular routes. For a challenging experience, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029988991/samartin-puerto-san-lorenzo-alto-de-la-farrapona-puerto-de-ventana-cueva-huerta-circular-through-the-parque-natural-de-somiedo" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Samartín, Puerto San Lorenzo, Alto de La Farrapona, Puerto de Ventana, Cueva Huerta — circular through the Parque Natural de Somiedo&lt;/a&gt;, which covers nearly 78 km. Another popular loop is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029986724/puerto-de-ventana-and-puerto-de-san-lorenzo-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo Loop&lt;/a&gt;, a 60 km trail through challenging mountain passes.

Q: Where can I park my car when accessing touring cycling routes in Teverga?
A: For routes like the Senda del Oso, parking is generally available at various access points along the trail, particularly near the start and end points in towns like Proaza or Tuñón. For other mountain routes, parking can often be found in the villages or designated areas near the trailheads. It's advisable to check specific route details for recommended parking locations.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Teverga?
A: The best time for touring cycling in Teverga is typically from spring to autumn (May to October). During these months, the weather is generally milder and more stable, making for more enjoyable rides. Summer offers warmer temperatures, while spring and autumn provide pleasant conditions with vibrant foliage. Be aware that mountain passes can still be affected by snow or adverse weather outside of these peak seasons.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Teverga?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Reviewers often praise the region's stunning natural landscapes, the challenge of its mountain passes, and the unique experience of routes like the Senda del Oso. The diverse terrain, from dense beech forests to dramatic limestone formations, is frequently highlighted as a major draw.

Q: Are there opportunities to rent bicycles in Teverga?
A: Yes, bicycle hire is available locally, particularly for those looking to explore the popular Senda del Oso. This makes it convenient for visitors who don't bring their own bikes or wish to try out different types of cycling equipment.

Q: Can I spot wildlife while cycling in Teverga?
A: Teverga is known for its rich wildlife, being part of the Ubiñas-La Mesa Natural Park. The region boasts an 'inexhaustible habitat of wild life,' including deer, wild boar, and foxes. The Senda del Oso is famous for the opportunity to spot Cantabrian brown bears in semi-freedom at the Bear Enclosure near Proaza, making it a unique wildlife viewing experience.

Q: Are there any cafes or places to eat along the cycling routes?
A: Along popular routes like the Senda del Oso, you'll find small villages and towns that offer cafes, restaurants, and pubs where you can refuel and rest. For more remote mountain routes, it's advisable to carry sufficient food and water, as amenities may be scarce. Checking route maps for villages or towns along your chosen path is recommended.
tips: A really nice stretch of road. It is not usually very busy with motor vehicles, even so, be alert at every curve: with such beauty it is easy to get lost and change lanes.

Puerto de Ventana is a mountain pass located in the Cantabrian Mountains, between the Principality of Asturias and the province of León. It lies at an altitude of 1,587 meters and connects the municipality of San Emiliano in León with the Asturian municipality of Teverga.

From the pass, you can enjoy a breathtaking view of the San Emiliano Valley, known as Babia de Yuso.
Spectacular port that separates the Councils of Teverga and Somiedo. The place is ideal for doing several routes, most of them on the Camino Real de la Mesa.

faqs.question: How difficult is the Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo Loop for touring cyclists?
faqs.answer: This loop is classified as challenging to very difficult. Both the Ventana Pass and San Lorenzo Pass feature significant elevation gains and steep gradients, with sections reaching 14-15%. It requires good physical condition and appropriate gearing.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: The route offers consistently picturesque scenery. You'll pass through the stunning Somiedo Natural Park and have views of the Ubiñas Natural Park. Key highlights include the challenging ascents of Ventana Pass and San Lorenzo Pass, both renowned for their beauty. You might also encounter the Huerta Cave.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this loop?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this loop is generally from late spring to early autumn. During these months, the weather is more stable, and the mountain passes are typically free of snow. Be prepared for varied conditions, as mountain weather can change quickly.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: While the exact starting point for a loop can vary, a common access point is from the San Martín side, which is a good base for tackling the climbs. Look for parking options in or around San Martín de Teverga, which provides convenient access to both passes.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this cycling route?
faqs.answer: No specific permits or entrance fees are required to cycle this route. The loop traverses or offers views of the Somiedo Natural Park and Ubiñas Natural Park, but access to the roads themselves is generally free. Always respect local regulations and natural park guidelines.

faqs.question: Is this cycling loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route is on public roads, it is primarily designed for touring cyclists. If you plan to bring a dog, ensure it is accustomed to cycling alongside you and is kept on a leash, especially given the potential for wildlife and occasional livestock on the roads. Always prioritize your dog's safety and the safety of others.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through areas rich in biodiversity, particularly within the Somiedo Natural Park. It's common to see horses and cows grazing freely on the roads, especially on the San Lorenzo Pass. Keep an eye out for other local fauna, but always maintain a respectful distance.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or rest stops along the way?
faqs.answer: Given the remote nature of some sections, especially on the mountain passes, dedicated cafes or rest stops are not frequent. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks. You may find small villages or towns near the base of the passes or along connecting roads where you can refuel.

faqs.question: Does this loop connect with other official cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with several official trails. You will encounter parts of the Transcantábrica, Camín Real de la Mesa, [CIMA AS01] Ventana, and [CIMA AS06] San Lorenzo * La Plaza (Teverga), among others. These intersections can offer opportunities for extending or modifying your ride.

faqs.question: What makes this cycling loop particularly notable?
faqs.answer: This loop is notable for its inclusion of two iconic mountain passes, Puerto de Ventana and Puerto de San Lorenzo, both frequently featured in the prestigious Vuelta a España. This gives cyclists a chance to ride on routes steeped in professional cycling history, all while enjoying the stunning natural beauty of Asturias' mountain landscapes.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenges or tips for tackling the climbs on this loop?
faqs.answer: Both passes present significant challenges. The ascent of Puerto de San Lorenzo from the San Martín side has an average gradient of 8.0% over 11.2 km, with sections up to 15%. For Puerto de Ventana, the Asturian side (from San Martín de Teverga) is harder, with a 20.0 km climb at a 5.6% average gradient and an 11% maximum slope. Ensure your bike is equipped with appropriate gearing for sustained climbing, and be prepared for fast descents, especially on San Lorenzo which has fewer hairpin bends.
